_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.basket: Each line is the global price of one breakfast staple, indexed so that 2015 = 100, which lets you compare a pound of coffee with a tonne of wheat on the same axis. Averaged into a single basket, the cost of the raw materials in your morning is running around 91% above where it sat a decade ago. Two things stand out: the climb is recent and steep (most of it since 2021), and it is wildly uneven — staples have not moved together.
notes.movers: The damage is concentrated. Orange juice and coffee have roughly doubled-and-more, while wheat and sugar are up far more modestly. These aren't really inflation stories; they're weather-and-disease stories — citrus greening and hurricanes in Florida and Brazil for orange juice, frost and drought in the big arabica regions for coffee. Soft commodities like these are thin markets where one bad harvest in the wrong place moves the world price.
notes.why: So the headline 'your breakfast costs more' hides a more interesting truth: a couple of weather-exposed crops are doing the heavy lifting, not a broad commodity surge. It's a small, tangible reminder that a lot of everyday prices trace back to a handful of fields and the weather over them — and that those inputs can stay elevated for years while a crop recovers.
